proposed_from.topic: Flagship generalization of the banked network-exposure partial-ID result: sharp partial identification of complier direct and spillover effects when both the treatment exposure mapping and the instrumental exposure mapping may be misspecified, bridging exposure-mapping robustness with IV noncompliance and producing a computable finite-network sharp bound plus a small worked witness.
proposed_from.novelty_justification: {"repo_axis":"The closest in-catalogue result is the banked parent pid_network_exposure/v1, whose headline kernel is sharp ADE bounds from one shared hidden-edge completion under treatment exposure-map misspecification. This draft is not a relabelling of that theorem: it adds a latent take-up table indexed by instrumental exposure state, a second misspecified exposure map, and a new focal object -- the shared completion-principal-strata polytope together with the compatibility-deficit scalar against a triple-kernel product relaxation. The adjacent banked pid_dynamic_iv_compliance/v2 entry contributes finite compliance-mixture logic but no network exposure geometry, and the failed pid_network_random_set/v1 track contributes only witness discipline, not the same kernel. No supplied in-repo artifact already combines hidden-edge completion, dual exposure-map misspecification, and monotone compliance in one finite partial-ID statement.","published_axis":"The closest published threads are Hoshino-Yanagi (2024), Schroder-Oprescu-Feuerriegel-Kallus (2026), Imai-Jiang-Malani (2021), and Vazquez-Bare (2023). Hoshino-Yanagi give the IV-interference/noncompliance side but not a finite sharp identified set under dual exposure-map misspecification. Schroder et al. give sharp misspecified-exposure bounds but only for treatment exposure on a known network and without latent IV compliance. Imai-Jiang-Malani give the clean point-identified complier benchmark under two-stage randomization, and Vazquez-Bare explains why standard IV spillover contrasts aggregate latent effects under weaker conditions. The epsilon-gap is therefore specific and two-sided: no cited paper combines one shared hidden completion, two misspecified exposure maps, monotone compliance types, and a computable finite-network strict-gap witness."}
seed_list: Upgrade move 1: starting from pid_network_exposure/v1, replace treatment-equals-assignment by a monotone latent take-up table and a second misspecified instrumental exposure map, and conjecture that one shared completion-principal-strata polytope gives sharp finite-network bounds for complier direct and spillover effects.

Upgrade move 2: starting from the parent strict-containment phenomenon, conjecture that the product relaxation over treatment-exposure kernels, instrument-exposure kernels, and compliance kernels is strictly nonsharp on an open class of finite encouraged networks.

Upgrade move 3: starting from Imai-Jiang-Malani's identified complier direct and spillover effects, conjecture that one shared finite-network polytope jointly sharp-identifies the direct/spillover family under dual mapping misspecification, while coordinatewise endpoint stitching is nonsharp.

Upgrade move 4: starting from Nibbering-Oosterveen's policy-relevant IV effects under interference, ask for necessary-and-sufficient conditions under which exposure-robust complier bounds imply policy-robust intervention bounds on a finite network.
proposed_from.last_draft_handoff: {"status":"completed","mode":"pivot","version":1,"message":"Upgrade the parent shared-completion ADE bound to a shared completion-principal-strata polytope for complier direct and spillover effects under dual exposure-map misspecification, with a conjectured positive compatibility deficit on a four-unit witness.","upgrade_mode":true,"parent_qid":"pid_network_exposure","parent_spec":"v1","upgrade_axis":"generalization","delta_summary":"Relative to parent pid_network_exposure/v1, this draft widens the assumption class from treatment-equals-assignment with one misspecified treatment exposure map to randomized encouragement with monotone noncompliance and a second misspecified instrumental exposure map. The strict parent reduction is explicit in Corollary 1: when take-up equals encouragement and instrumental exposure is degenerate, the new shared completion-principal-strata polytope collapses to the parent's shared hidden-edge LP. The upgrade is not a relabelling because the new focal object is the joint completion-principal-strata polytope and its compatibility-deficit scalar, which tracks losses from forgetting that hidden edges jointly constrain instrumental exposure, treatment exposure, and complier status.","reused_bibkeys":["savje_2024_misspecified_exposure_mappings","schroder_oprescu_feuerriegel_kallus_2026_networks_partialid","weinstein_nevo_2026_misspecified_network"],"new_bibkeys":["hoshino_yanagi_2024_noncompliance_unknown_interference","imai_jiang_malani_2021_interference_noncompliance","vazquez_bare_2023_iv_spillovers","nibbering_oosterveen_2025_iv_interference_policy","acerenza_martinez_iriarte_sanchez_becerra_spini_2025_household_bounds"],"literature_map":"Verified reused parent anchors: Savje (2024, Biometrika) argues that exposure definitions and identifying assumptions should be separated; this still supports a dual-map robustness question. Schroder, Oprescu, Feuerriegel, and Kallus (2026, arXiv) still claim sharp upper and lower bounds for direct and spillover effects under misspecified treatment exposure mappings, but not IV compliance. Weinstein and Nevo (2026, Biometrics) still frame misspecified network structure as distinct from exposure-map misspecification, which keeps the parent completion logic relevant. New frontier anchor: Hoshino and Yanagi (2024, JASA) introduce instrumental exposure mappings for noncompliance with unknown interference and explicitly allow exposure-map misspecification, but stop at identification and IPW estimation rather than finite-network sharp bounds. Imai, Jiang, and Malani (2021, JASA) remain the clean point-ID benchmark for complier direct and spillover effects under two-stage randomization. Vazquez-Bare (2023, JASA) remains the warning that ordinary IV spillover contrasts mix multiple latent effects and become interpretable only under stronger compliance structure. Acerenza et al. (2025, arXiv) show sharp household encouragement bounds, suggesting that finite polyhedral sharpness is plausible but not solved for many-peer networks. Nibbering and Oosterveen (2025, arXiv) add a policy-relevant IV-interference benchmark, which gives the downstream consumer for a sharp complier envelope. The active tension is now explicit: treatment-map robustness papers lack IV compliance, while IV-interference papers lack finite sharp joint misspecification bounds with a shared completion witness.","seeds":["Upgrade move 1: replace the parent ADE LP by a shared completion-principal-strata polytope that sharply bounds complier direct and spillover effects when both treatment and instrumental exposure maps may be misspecified.","Upgrade move 2: prove that the product relaxation over treatment-exposure kernels, instrumental-exposure kernels, and compliance kernels is strictly nonsharp on an open class of finite encouraged networks.","Upgrade move 3: derive one joint support-function bound for the full complier direct/spillover family, so direct and spillover endpoints cannot be stitched coordinatewise from separate relaxations.","Upgrade move 4: characterize when exposure-robust complier bounds imply policy-relevant IV intervention bounds on a finite network, and isolate the failure mode when they do not.","Upgrade move 5: introduce a compatibility-deficit scalar for dual-map network IV misspecification and conjecture that a four-unit hub witness already yields a positive computable gap between the shared-completion bound and the product relaxation."],"seed_details":[{"one_line":"Upgrade move 1: replace the parent ADE LP by a shared seed_details.one_line: Upgrade move 1: starting from pid_network_exposure/v1, replace treatment-equals-assignment by a monotone latent take-up table and a second misspecified instrumental exposure map, and conjecture that one shared completion-principal-strata polytope gives sharp finite-network bounds for complier direct and spillover effects.

anchor_paper.author: Hoshino and Yanagi
anchor_paper.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
anchor_paper.claim: Introduces instrumental exposure mappings for network IV with noncompliance and unknown interference, but not finite-network sharp bounds under joint treatment/instrument exposure misspecification.
anchor_paper.verbatim_quote: We investigate identification conditions for the intention-to-treat effects and the average treatment effects for compliers

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Generalization is stronger because the new object is not just a broader class: the same hidden edge changes both compliance status and outcome exposure, creating a new compatibility obstruction.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from hoshino_yanagi_2024_noncompliance_unknown_interference plus schroder_oprescu_feuerriegel_kallus_2026_networks_partialid because one can just stack their constraints.
seed_details.rebuttal: False: neither paper has one shared latent graph generating both instrumental and treatment exposures, so the stacked product system misses the cross-channel feasibility geometry.

adversarial_referee.instance: Headline names a bound but no focal object beyond an LP.
adversarial_referee.rebuttal_or_downgrade: Rebuttal: the focal object is the joint completion-principal-strata polytope, exhibited in Section 9 on a four-unit network.

adversarial_referee.instance: Why only a direct effect if the same primitives also identify spillovers?
adversarial_referee.rebuttal_or_downgrade: Rebuttal: Conjecture 2 explicitly promotes the kernel to the direct/spillover family and policy-weighted combinations.

adversarial_referee.instance: Hoshino-Yanagi already handles misspecified exposure mappings under unknown interference.
adversarial_referee.rebuttal_or_downgrade: Rebuttal: yes for identification/IPW with instrumental exposure mappings, no for finite-network sharp bounds with simultaneous treatment-map misspecification.

seed_details.one_line: Upgrade move 2: starting from the parent strict-containment phenomenon, conjecture that the product relaxation over treatment-exposure kernels, instrument-exposure kernels, and compliance kernels is strictly nonsharp on an open class of finite encouraged networks.

anchor_paper.author: pid_network_exposure prior proposal / reviewer trail
anchor_paper.claim: The parent banked result already isolates strict nonsharpness for treatment-exposure misspecification; the upgrade asks whether the same incompatibility survives with IV compliance types.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Counterexample search is strong here because the refuted object is named published-style sensitivity practice, not a self-invented shortcut, and the witness is promoted to an open class.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is just the parent witness with extra notation because compliance can be set to perfect compliance.
seed_details.rebuttal: False if proved: the IV version must show the same hidden edge jointly constrains compliance and exposure, not only treatment exposure; the witness must therefore be genuinely dual-channel.

adversarial_referee.instance: Strict gap may rely on a hand-tuned observable law with collapsed cells.
adversarial_referee.rebuttal_or_downgrade: Rebuttal: the draft builds the claim around an open neighborhood of the four-unit witness, not one isolated table.

adversarial_referee.instance: Claims a sharpness-failure motif but only exhibits one finite witness.
adversarial_referee.rebuttal_or_downgrade: Rebuttal: Conjecture 1 is explicitly open-set nonsharpness, and the proof route includes promotion from the witness to an open class.

adversarial_referee.instance: The product relaxation is not a published estimator or theorem.
adversarial_referee.rebuttal_or_downgrade: Rebuttal: the comparator chain runs through Schroder-style cellwise sensitivity logic and the parent banked relaxation, both named and documented.

seed_details.one_line: Upgrade move 3: starting from Imai-Jiang-Malani's identified complier direct and spillover effects, conjecture that one shared finite-network polytope jointly sharp-identifies the direct/spillover family under dual mapping misspecification, while coordinatewise endpoint stitching is nonsharp.

anchor_paper.author: Imai, Jiang, and Malani
anchor_paper.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
anchor_paper.claim: Identifies complier direct and spillover effects in two-stage randomized experiments under stratified interference.
anchor_paper.verbatim_quote: we establish the nonparametric identification of the complier average direct and spillover effects

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: This avoids the ATT/CATE anti-pattern because the new content is a joint identified region from one structural object, not a re-labeling of one estimand.
seed_details.referee_objection: Joint identification is immediate once each coordinate is separately bounded.
seed_details.rebuttal: False in partial ID: separate endpoint programs need not be jointly attainable from one latent graph/compliance structure, so the rectangle can exceed the true image.

adversarial_referee.instance: Why should a flagship referee care about one extra coordinate?
adversarial_referee.rebuttal_or_downgrade: Rebuttal: the theorem is about the family as the unit of identification, not an added coordinate; the same feasible set determines all coordinates jointly.

adversarial_referee.instance: Proves a sufficient common-polytope representation but not why separate bounding is wrong.
adversarial_referee.rebuttal_or_downgrade: Rebuttal: the draft makes nonrectangularity part of Conjecture 2 rather than stopping at a sufficient representation.

adversarial_referee.instance: Imai-Jiang-Malani already defines the target family.
adversarial_referee.rebuttal_or_downgrade: Rebuttal: yes under stratified interference and point-ID structure, not under finite-network dual misspecification and sharp partial ID.

seed_details.one_line: Upgrade move 4: starting from Nibbering-Oosterveen's policy-relevant IV effects under interference, ask for necessary-and-sufficient conditions under which exposure-robust complier bounds imply policy-robust intervention bounds on a finite network.

anchor_paper.author: Nibbering and Oosterveen
anchor_paper.claim: Defines and partially identifies policy-relevant direct and spillover effects with IVs under interference.
anchor_paper.verbatim_quote: This paper defines and partially identifies direct and spillover effects with a clear policy-relevant interpretation

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: This is weaker because the policy bridge risks becoming a qualitative implication theorem unless one names a computable condition or counterexample frontier.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is policy rhetoric layered on top of the same bound object.
seed_details.rebuttal: Partly lands; without a named necessary-and-sufficient condition or sharp counterexample class, the move is below the chosen flagship angle.

adversarial_referee.instance: No named focal mathematical object yet.
Promises a policy-relevance criterion without the necessity direction.
Could collapse to one more sufficient condition for policy relevance.

proposed_from.literature_map:
Verified reused parent anchors: Aronow and Samii (2017, AOAS, AronowSamii2017) gives the randomization-plus-exposure-mapping benchmark for known interference form; Savje (2024, Biometrika, savje_2024_misspecified_exposure_mappings) separates exposure definitions from identifying assumptions and explicitly leaves open what restrictions replace a correct mapping; Schroder, Oprescu, Feuerriegel, and Kallus (2026, arXiv, schroder_oprescu_feuerriegel_kallus_2026_networks_partialid) gives sharp treatment-exposure misspecification bounds with known network and no IV compliance layer.
New frontier anchors for the upgrade: Hoshino and Yanagi (2024, JASA, hoshino_yanagi_2024_noncompliance_unknown_interference) studies noncompliance and unknown interference with instrumental exposure mappings, but through identification and IPW rather than finite-network sharp bounds; Imai, Jiang, and Malani (2021, JASA, imai_jiang_malani_2021_interference_noncompliance) identifies complier direct and spillover effects under stratified interference, not dual exposure-map misspecification; Vazquez-Bare (2023, JASA, vazquez_bare_2023_iv_spillovers) shows standard IV spillover contrasts mix compliance-type-specific effects, motivating a sharp latent-type object; Acerenza et al. (2025, arXiv, acerenza_martinez_iriarte_sanchez_becerra_spini_2025_household_bounds) gives sharp encouragement-design bounds in two-person settings, suggesting a many-peer finite-network extension; Nibbering and Oosterveen (2025, arXiv, nibbering_oosterveen_2025_iv_interference_policy) partially identifies policy-relevant IV effects under interference, creating a downstream consumer for a computable network-IV sharp set.
Core tension: Savje/Schroder make exposure-map misspecification first-class, while Hoshino/Imai/Vazquez-Bare make noncompliance under interference first-class; no cited paper combines both layers with one shared latent network completion. Parent banked prior art already shows shared hidden-edge compatibility can make a cellwise treatment-exposure relaxation nonsharp; the open flagship move is to prove the same phenomenon once the same hidden edge also changes instrumental exposure and complier status.

banked_reason: Stage -0.5 NO-PASS after 5 angles: network-IV exposure upgrade repeatedly had a plausible literature gap but failed flagship proposal review on uncomputed/infeasible witnesses, promissory transport/frontier objects, and LP well-posedness.
